Simulated Consulting Engagement — Monday.com

Operations Command Center

An 11-board Monday.com workspace built for a fictional management-consulting client, designed the way I'd build it for a real one: one connected system for executive oversight, delivery, risk, client management, and team capacity — instead of eleven disconnected spreadsheets.

Client (Simulated)
Apex Global Consulting Ltd
Role Simulated
Executive Assistant / Ops Coordinator
Duration
8 Weeks — May 4 to Jun 30, 2026
Self-directed practice engagement — no real client — built to demonstrate workspace design at consulting scale.
Workspace Ledger
11interconnected boards across 5 functional clusters
16distinct view types in use (table, action timeline, calendar, capacity timeline, chart, dashboard, executive kpi line chart, gantt view, kanban, onboarding timeline, operations timeline, portfolio gantt, procurement timeline, risk timeline, timeline, workload)
18+automation rules configured across 7 boards
92.8composite executive KPI score, dashboard-computed
$59,830tracked spend rolled up on the budget dashboard
The Brief

Why simulate a consultancy instead of building another to-do board.

Most Monday.com portfolio pieces are a single board. Real operations work rarely is. I set the scenario at a mid-size consulting firm on purpose — it forces the same problems a remote EA or ops coordinator actually gets hired to solve: leadership needs one view, delivery teams need another, and none of it is useful if the boards don't talk to each other.

Apex Global Consulting Ltd is fictional. The workspace, the board architecture, the automations, and the dashboards are real — built solo, end to end, over eight weeks.

System Map

Eleven boards, five functional clusters.

Every board below is cross-linked through Connect-Board columns, so a task, a risk, and a client contract can all point back to the same project.

  • Executive Operations Dashboard
  • Executive KPI Dashboard
  • Executive Document Register

The engine room — projects, tasks, and meetings, all cross-linked.

  • Project Portfolio Management
  • Task Management System
  • Executive Meetings & Action Tracker

Risk & Governance

The paper trail that keeps a consultancy defensible.

  • Enterprise Risk & Issue Register
  • SOP & Knowledge Base

Client & Finance

Revenue-facing operations: who's paying, and what it costs to serve them.

  • Client Onboarding & CRM
  • Budget, Expenses & Procurement Tracker

People & Capacity

Making sure delivery promises match who's actually available.

  • Resource & Team Capacity Management
Strategic Oversight

Where a COO or client principal would open the workspace first.

Executive Operations Dashboard

Main tableOperations TimelineDashboard

The landing view for leadership: current performance metrics, active projects, issues requiring attention, and completed initiatives, grouped so a COO can scan status in under a minute.

Grouped-table design for executive scanning — status, priority, and progress rolled into one screen instead of scattered across boards.

Executive KPI Dashboard

Main tableExecutive KPI Line ChartCalendarDashboard2 automations

A composite KPI score (92.8) built from a Numbers widget, a Battery widget tracking steady/up/down trend counts, and a current-vs-previous-value bar chart comparing this period's metrics against the last.

Formula-driven dashboard widgets (Numbers, Battery, comparison charts) that turn raw board data into a single glanceable executive metric.

Executive Document Register

Main table2 automations

Version-controlled registry for contracts, policies, reports, financial documents, presentations, and templates — each with expiry date, review date, version number, and document ID for audit-readiness.

Document-lifecycle tracking (version, expiry, review cadence) — the records-management discipline an EA is expected to own for an executive.

Delivery & Execution

The engine room — projects, tasks, and meetings, all cross-linked.

Project Portfolio Management

Main tablePortfolio GanttTimelineKanbanChart5 automations

Active, planned, completed, and at-risk projects tracked with owner, department, status, priority, budget, and risk level — e.g. the Digital Transformation Launch (PRJ-001), In Progress, Critical priority, Medium risk, 85% complete, $30,000 budget.

Portfolio-level views (Gantt, Kanban, Timeline) built from the same underlying data — one dataset, four ways to read it depending on who's looking.

Task Management System

Main tableGantt ViewKanbanCalendarWorkload

Tasks grouped by This Week, Upcoming, Waiting/Blocked, and Completed, each carrying a Task ID, linked project, department, priority, and status — e.g. 'Prepare COO Weekly Meeting Agenda', Critical priority, Working on it.

Dependency and workload visibility — a Workload view to catch overallocation before it becomes a missed deadline.

Executive Meetings & Action Tracker

Main tableAction TimelineCalendarChart2 automations

Every meeting tracked from Upcoming through This Week, Awaiting Follow-up, and Completed — with agenda-sent, minutes-completed, and follow-up-required columns so nothing slips between meetings.

The exact discipline an Executive Assistant is hired for: meeting prep, minutes, and follow-up tracked as data, not memory.

Risk & Governance

The paper trail that keeps a consultancy defensible.

Enterprise Risk & Issue Register

Main tableRisk TimelineCalendarChart2 automations

A RAID-style register grouping High, Medium, and Low priority risks plus active and closed issues — e.g. 'Vendor Delivery Delays', High probability, High impact, Critical overall risk, with a mitigation plan and contingency plan on file. A risk-timeline view plots deadlines against the calendar week, and a chart view breaks down open risk by severity.

RAID-log thinking translated into a live Monday.com board — probability × impact scoring, mitigation plans, and a visual severity breakdown for governance reporting.

SOP & Knowledge Base

Main table2 automations

SOPs organized by department (Executive Administration, Operations, Finance, Procurement, Client Management, Project Management), each with a SOP ID, version number, review date, and approval status.

Process-governance structure — every SOP versioned and dated for review, the same habit built into the Hale Limited operations practice.

Client & Finance

Revenue-facing operations: who's paying, and what it costs to serve them.

Client Onboarding & CRM

Main tableOnboarding TimelineKanbanCalendarChartWorkload

A full pipeline from New Leads through Proposal Sent, Contract Signed, Onboarding, and Active Clients — e.g. Bright Tech moving through Contract Signed at Critical priority with a $65,000 contract value and a Healthy health-status flag. The Kanban view mirrors the same pipeline for a faster drag-and-drop read; the chart view counts clients at each stage.

CRM pipeline design with health-status flags and contract values attached — client-facing operations, not just an internal task list.

Budget, Expenses & Procurement Tracker

Main tableProcurement TimelineChartCalendarDashboard3 automations

Budget allocation, purchase requests, purchase orders, expenses, and reconciliation tracked with variance columns — e.g. a Dell Technologies laptop purchase order for $9,250, fully approved and paid. A dashboard rolls this up into a Battery completion widget and a Numbers widget totalling $59,830 tracked.

Procurement-to-payment tracking with automatic variance calculation and an executive dashboard summarizing total spend at a glance.

People & Capacity

Making sure delivery promises match who's actually available.

Resource & Team Capacity Management

Main tableCapacity TimelineChartWorkload

Staffing capacity by department (Leadership, Operations, Marketing, IT, Finance, Procurement), each person tracked against a capacity percentage and hours/week — the data an ops coordinator needs before promising a delivery date.

Resource-capacity modelling — checking who's actually available before a project timeline gets committed to a client.

Monday.com Proficiency

What building this actually required.

Not just filling in a table — the parts of Monday.com that separate a board from a system.

Workspace & board architecture

  • 11 boards structured around functional ownership, not just topic
  • Connect-Board columns linking tasks, risks, and clients back to a single project record
  • Grouped views (status, priority, department) so the same board reads differently for different audiences

Views & visual reporting

  • Action Timeline, Calendar, Capacity Timeline, Chart, Dashboard, Executive KPI Line Chart, Gantt View, Kanban, Onboarding Timeline, Operations Timeline, Portfolio Gantt, Procurement Timeline, Risk Timeline, Timeline, Workload configured across boards depending on what the data needed to show
  • Chart views for instant severity/stage breakdowns (risk by priority, CRM by pipeline stage)
  • Dashboard widgets — Battery, Numbers, Data-over-time, Gantt — aggregating multiple boards into one executive screen

Automation & workflow logic

  • 18+ automation rules live across 7 boards (status changes, notifications, recurring reminders)
  • Formula-driven KPI rollups rather than manually updated numbers
  • Variance columns on the budget tracker calculating automatically from budget vs. actual cost

Governance & documentation

  • RAID-style risk register with probability × impact scoring and mitigation plans
  • Version-controlled SOP and document registers with review dates and approval status
  • Structure designed to survive an audit, not just look organized
Beyond Monday.com

The system, not the software.

The part of this project that transfers isn't the Monday.com interface — it's the underlying operating logic: separate the executive view from the working view, link everything back to one source record, and put a review date on anything that governs a decision. That logic is what I'd bring to any platform a client already runs on.

In practice that means the same board architecture rebuilt in Asana or ClickUp for delivery tracking, a Notion knowledge base for SOPs and documentation, or a Trello board for a lighter-weight client pipeline — and AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot) used throughout to draft the SOPs, risk descriptions, and reporting templates that sit inside these boards.

Monday.com Asana ClickUp Trello Notion Microsoft Project Google Workspace ChatGPT · Claude · Gemini · Copilot
